diseases.name: Influenza (Flu)
symptoms: Severe body aches and muscle pain
causes: Infection by influenza viruses (types A, B, C, and D)
Transmitted through respiratory droplets when infected people cough, sneeze, or talk
Can spread by touching contaminated surfaces then touching face
treatments: Rest and plenty of sleep to help immune system fight infection
Drink fluids to prevent dehydration (water, warm tea, soup)
Over-the-counter pain relievers for fever and aches
Antiviral medications if prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset
Humidifiers to ease breathing and soothe airways
Avoid contact with others to prevent spread
precautions: Get annual flu vaccine, especially if in high-risk groups
Wash hands frequently with soap and water for at least 20 seconds
Avoid touching eyes, nose, and mouth
Cover coughs and sneezes with tissue or elbow
Clean and disinfect frequently touched surfaces
Avoid close contact with sick individuals
Stay home when sick to prevent spreading to others
Maintain good overall health through diet, exercise, and sleep

diseases.name: Type 2 Diabetes
symptoms: Darkened skin areas, especially in armpits and neck
Tingling or numbness in hands or feet
causes: Insulin resistance - cells don't respond properly to insulin
Pancreas cannot produce enough insulin to overcome resistance
Obesity and excess body fat, especially abdominal fat
Poor diet high in processed foods and sugars
treatments: Lifestyle modifications including diet changes and exercise
Blood sugar monitoring to track glucose levels
Oral medications to improve insulin sensitivity
Regular physical activity - at least 150 minutes per week
Regular medical checkups and HbA1c testing
precautions: Maintain healthy weight through balanced diet
Engage in regular physical activity
Eat high-fiber, low-glycemic foods
Limit refined carbohydrates and sugary beverages
Monitor blood pressure and cholesterol levels
Quit smoking if applicable
Get regular health screenings, especially if family history exists
Manage stress through meditation or yoga
Ensure adequate sleep (7-9 hours per night)

diseases.name: Hypertension (High Blood Pressure)
symptoms: Often called 'silent killer' - many have no symptoms
Pounding in chest, neck, or ears
causes: Primary hypertension - develops gradually with no identifiable cause
Secondary hypertension - caused by underlying conditions
treatments: Medications including ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, or diuretics
Reduce sodium intake to less than 2,300 mg per day
DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension)
Regular blood pressure monitoring at home
Medication compliance and regular doctor visits
precautions: Eat a heart-healthy diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains
Reduce sodium in diet
Maintain healthy weight
Exercise regularly - at least 30 minutes most days
Limit alcohol to moderate amounts
Don't smoke or quit if you do
Manage stress through relaxation techniques
Get regular blood pressure checks
Take prescribed medications consistently
Limit caffeine intake
Get adequate sleep

diseases.name: Asthma
symptoms: Wheezing when exhaling (common sign in children)
Trouble sleeping due to shortness of breath or coughing
Coughing or wheezing attacks worsened by respiratory virus
Symptoms worsen with cold air or exercise
causes: Airway inflammation and narrowing of bronchial tubes
Environmental allergens (pollen, dust mites, mold, pet dander)
Air pollutants and irritants (smoke, chemical fumes)
treatments: Allergy medications if triggered by allergens
Use peak flow meter to monitor lung function
precautions: Identify and avoid asthma triggers
Keep rescue inhaler accessible at all times
Monitor breathing with peak flow meter
Follow asthma action plan
Get vaccinated against flu and pneumonia
Use air purifiers to reduce indoor allergens
Keep home clean and free of dust
Avoid smoking and secondhand smoke
Manage stress and anxiety
Warm up before exercise
Cover nose and mouth in cold weather
Take medications as prescribed

diseases.name: Migraine
symptoms: Intense throbbing or pulsing pain, usually on one side of head
Visual disturbances (aura) - flashing lights, blind spots
Pain that worsens with physical activity
causes: Changes in brainstem and its interactions with trigeminal nerve
Imbalances in brain chemicals, including serotonin
Hormonal changes, especially in women (menstruation, pregnancy)
Certain foods (aged cheeses, processed meats, alcohol)
Sensory stimuli (bright lights, loud sounds, strong smells)
Sleep changes (too much or too little sleep)
treatments: Cold or warm compresses on head or neck
Caffeine in small amounts may help
precautions: Keep a headache diary to identify triggers
Maintain regular sleep schedule
Eat regular meals, don't skip
Stay hydrated
Manage stress through relaxation techniques
Exercise regularly but avoid sudden intense activity
Limit caffeine and alcohol
Avoid known food triggers
Use sunglasses in bright light
Maintain good posture
Take breaks from screens
Don't overuse pain medications (can cause rebound headaches)

diseases.name: Anxiety Disorders
symptoms: Sleep disturbances (difficulty falling or staying asleep)
causes: Complex interaction of genetic, environmental, and psychological factors
Traumatic life events or chronic stress
Medical conditions (heart disease, thyroid problems, diabetes)
precautions: Practice stress management techniques daily
Maintain regular sleep schedule
Engage in regular physical activity
Limit caffeine, alcohol, and nicotine
Eat balanced, nutritious meals
Stay connected with supportive friends and family
Learn to identify and challenge negative thoughts
Practice relaxation techniques regularly
Set realistic goals and expectations
Take breaks and practice self-care
Avoid self-medication with alcohol or drugs
Seek professional help when symptoms interfere with daily life

diseases.name: COVID-19 (Coronavirus Disease)
symptoms: Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
New loss of taste or smell
causes: Transmitted through respiratory droplets from infected person
Asymptomatic carriers can spread the virus
Variants of the virus may have different transmission rates
treatments: Most cases are mild and can be managed at home
Stay hydrated with plenty of fluids
Over-the-counter medications for fever and pain
Monitor oxygen levels with pulse oximeter if available
Seek emergency care if: trouble breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, inability to stay awake, bluish lips/face
precautions: Get vaccinated and stay up-to-date with boosters
Wear masks in crowded indoor spaces
Maintain physical distancing (6 feet) when possible
Wash hands frequently for at least 20 seconds
Use hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol
Avoid touching face, especially eyes, nose, mouth
Improve ventilation in indoor spaces
Clean and disinfect frequently touched surfaces
Stay home when sick
Get tested if exposed or symptomatic
Follow local health guidelines
Avoid crowded, poorly ventilated spaces

diseases.name: Osteoarthritis
symptoms: Joint pain during or after movement
Joint stiffness, especially in the morning or after inactivity
Tenderness when applying light pressure to joint
Loss of flexibility and range of motion
Bone spurs (extra bits of bone) around affected joint
causes: Breakdown of cartilage that cushions joints
Aging - wear and tear over time
Repetitive stress on joints from occupation or sports
Obesity - excess weight puts stress on joints
Gender - women more likely to develop osteoarthritis
treatments: Physical therapy to strengthen muscles around joints
Occupational therapy to modify daily activities
Weight loss to reduce stress on joints
Exercise - low-impact activities like swimming, walking
Joint replacement surgery in severe cases
precautions: Maintain healthy weight to reduce joint stress
Stay active with regular low-impact exercise
Protect joints from injury during activities
Use proper body mechanics when lifting or carrying
Strengthen muscles around joints
Maintain good posture
Avoid prolonged repetitive movements
Use assistive devices when needed
Wear supportive, cushioned shoes
Apply ice after activities to reduce inflammation
Pace activities and take regular breaks
Consider swimming or water aerobics for joint-friendly exercise

metadata.disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and should not be used for self-diagnosis or treatment. Always consult with a qualified healthcare professional for medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
